findings.title: Cayman FATCA and CRS reporting deadline: July 31, 2026
findings.summary: Cayman Islands financial institutions have until July 31, 2026, to complete their FATCA reporting, CRS reporting, and CRS filing declaration for the 2025 reporting year. This deadline applies under the current transitional rules before the new June 30 deadline takes effect for the 2026 reporting period. Entities that became Financial Institutions in 2025 must register by April 30, 2026.
findings.client_impact: Cayman-domiciled financial institutions — including investment funds, feeder vehicles, and managed accounts — must complete FATCA and CRS returns for the 2025 reporting year by July 31, 2026, just 25 days away. Missing this deadline exposes clients to penalties under Cayman Tax Information Authority regulations and risks the fund's standing with the TIA.
findings.urgency_reason: Hard statutory filing deadline in 25 days. This is not a regulatory commentary item — it is an actionable compliance deadline for any client with Cayman fund interests or financial institution accounts that has not yet filed.

findings.title: BCB Resolution 575 expands eligible holders of foreign currency deposit accounts
findings.summary: On June 18, 2026, the Central Bank of Brazil enacted Resolution No. 575, amending BCB Resolutions 277 and 278 to allow new categories of legal entities to hold foreign currency deposit accounts in Brazil. Eligible entities must demonstrate existing external credit transactions or foreign direct investment, reported in the SCE-Crédito or SCE-IED systems. The resolution takes effect October 1, 2026, with monthly reporting obligations to the BCB.
findings.client_impact: Brazilian legal entities that hold foreign credit receivables or have made foreign direct investment can now qualify to open foreign currency deposit accounts in Brazil — a new treasury and liquidity management tool. Clients with Brazilian operating companies or holding structures bearing external credit exposure should assess eligibility and new monthly BCB reporting obligations before October 1.
findings.urgency_reason: Effective October 1, 2026 (~87 days). The structural change requires advance assessment of eligibility, registration in BCB systems (SCE-Crédito or SCE-IED), and preparation for monthly reporting. Clients need lead time to act with their Brazilian counsel.

findings.title: BCB bans stablecoins and crypto for eFX cross-border settlement, effective October 2026
findings.summary: Brazil's Central Bank banned electronic foreign exchange (eFX) providers from using stablecoins (including USDT) and other crypto assets (including Bitcoin) to settle overseas remittances. EFX payments must now use traditional foreign exchange transactions or non-resident real accounts. The ban was announced May 2026 with an October 1, 2026 effective date.
findings.client_impact: Clients currently using USDT, Bitcoin, or other crypto assets to settle cross-border remittances from Brazil through eFX providers must restructure their payment arrangements before October 1, 2026. After that date, all eFX settlements must go through traditional FX channels or non-resident real accounts; violations could expose clients to BCB sanctions.
findings.urgency_reason: Clear effective date of October 1, 2026 (~87 days) with material compliance risk for any Brazil-connected client currently using stablecoins for cross-border flows. Transition planning, including engaging traditional FX providers, requires several weeks of lead time.

findings.title: FinCEN BOI: domestic US companies fully exempt; foreign companies face new deadlines
findings.summary: FinCEN's interim final rule (March 26, 2025) revised the definition of 'reporting company' to include only entities formed under foreign law and registered in a US state. All US-formed entities and their beneficial owners are now exempt from BOI reporting under the Corporate Transparency Act. Foreign reporting companies must still file BOI but are not required to report US-person beneficial owners. A final rule is expected in 2026.
findings.client_impact: US-formed entities are now fully exempt from BOI reporting, removing a major compliance burden for clients with domestic US holding companies, LLCs, and corporations. However, foreign entities registered in US states remain subject to BOI reporting and must identify non-US beneficial owners — clients with offshore holding companies registered in Delaware or other states should confirm their continued filing obligations.
findings.urgency_reason: WATCH because a final rule is expected in 2026 that could further revise the framework, and the distinction between domestic-exempt and foreign-still-reporting entities creates ongoing confusion. Advisors must correctly classify each client entity before advising on BOI compliance.

findings.title: One Big Beautiful Bill Act: US federal estate tax exemption permanently raised to $15M per person
findings.summary: Signed into law July 4, 2025, the One Big Beautiful Bill Act permanently increased the unified estate, gift, and GST tax exemption to $15 million per person ($30 million per married couple) effective January 1, 2026, with annual inflation indexing from 2027. The 40% estate tax rate above the exemption is unchanged. The annual gift exclusion is $19,000 per donee for 2026. This eliminates the TCJA sunset that would have halved exemptions.
findings.client_impact: The permanent $15M exemption fundamentally changes the cost-benefit calculus for lifetime gifting strategies, spousal portability elections, and irrevocable trust structures for US-connected HNW clients. Clients whose estate plans were premised on defending against a TCJA sunset reversion to ~$7M should review whether existing SLATs, ILITs, or GRATs remain optimal or can be simplified.
findings.urgency_reason: WATCH because the law is already in force but many clients will not have revisited their pre-2026 estate planning documents. Advisors should proactively schedule estate plan reviews, particularly where structures were designed to hedge the now-eliminated sunset risk.

findings.title: Australia AML Tranche 2 reforms: lawyers, accountants, real estate agents required to comply from July 1, 2026
findings.summary: From July 1, 2026, Australia's Tranche 2 AML/CTF reforms came into force, requiring lawyers, accountants, real estate agents, and other designated non-financial businesses and professions (DNFBPs) to comply with AML/CTF obligations for the first time. This brings Australia into alignment with FATF standards and is relevant for Australian-connected wealth structures.
findings.client_impact: Any FGC client with Australian-connected structures or transactions will now face AML/KYC requests from their Australian lawyers, accountants, and real estate agents — previously exempt from such obligations. Clients engaged in Australian property transactions or advised by Australian professionals should be briefed on the new information requests they can expect to receive.
findings.urgency_reason: Already in force as of July 1, 2026. Clients in mid-transaction with Australian advisors may already be receiving new CDD requests without prior warning; advisors should brief affected clients immediately.

findings.title: Cayman CRS Regime 2.0 amendments effective January 1, 2026 with stricter data and deadline requirements
findings.summary: Significant amendments to Cayman's Tax Information Authority (CRS) Regulations came into force January 1, 2026. Key changes include: a new Principal Point of Contact (PPoC) must be locally appointed; annual CRS and FATCA returns (including NIL returns) and CRS Compliance Forms move to a June 30 deadline (first applying for the 2026 reporting year, due June 30, 2027); registration deadlines advance to January 31 from 2027. Reporting is expanding to cover digital assets.
findings.client_impact: Cayman funds must now have a locally resident Principal Point of Contact in place — an obligation already live — and should update their compliance calendars to reflect the June 30 annual return deadline starting in 2027, as well as the earlier January 31 registration deadline. Funds with digital asset holdings should engage their fund administrator now to assess expanded reporting scope.
findings.urgency_reason: WATCH because the PPoC appointment obligation is already in force and the January 31, 2027 registration deadline requires preparation well in advance. Fund administrators for Cayman vehicles need to confirm they have operationalized the new requirements.

findings.title: BVI Beneficial Ownership Register fully operational from April 1, 2026 with legitimate interest access
findings.summary: The BVI Beneficial Ownership Register became fully operational in April 2026. From April 1, 2026, any person may request inspection of, or a copy of, an entry in the BO Register by demonstrating a 'legitimate interest' to the Registrar in writing (with a fee). Exemptions were expanded to include subsidiaries of funds, companies listed on recognised stock exchanges, and BVI companies held by regulated trustees.
findings.client_impact: Any person can now request inspection of BVI beneficial ownership information by asserting a 'legitimate interest,' ending the effective confidentiality that BVI structures previously offered. HNW clients with BVI holding companies should urgently review whether regulatory-trustee, fund-subsidiary, or listed-company exemptions apply to their structure, and whether their ownership information is currently exposed.
findings.urgency_reason: WATCH because the register has been live since April 2026 and the access pathway is already operable. Clients who have not reviewed their BVI structure's exposure are already operating with an unassessed privacy risk.

findings.title: Cyprus comprehensive tax reform effective January 1, 2026: CIT raised to 15%, SDC reduced, crypto taxed at 8%
findings.summary: Approved December 22, 2025 and effective January 1, 2026, Cyprus's landmark tax reform package raises CIT from 12.5% to 15%, reduces SDC on actual dividends from 17% to 5% for post-2026 profits, abolishes deemed dividend distribution for post-2026 profits, introduces an 8% flat tax on crypto asset disposal gains, and raises the personal income tax-free threshold to €22,000. Non-domiciled individuals retain exemptions on worldwide dividend/interest income, capital gains, and no wealth/inheritance taxes for 17 years.
findings.client_impact: Clients with Cyprus operating companies or holding structures must model the net effect of higher CIT (now 15%) offset against lower SDC (5%) and the abolition of deemed dividend distribution — the overall burden will shift depending on their dividend policy. The non-dom regime remains highly attractive for HNW individuals taking up Cyprus residency, and the new 8% flat crypto gains tax provides planning clarity for crypto-holding clients.
findings.urgency_reason: WATCH because the reform has been in force since January 1, 2026, and many clients will not have modeled the net impact or revisited their Cyprus dividend flows and corporate structures in light of the changes. Active review is warranted.

findings.title: DIFC Variable Capital Company (VCC) Regulations enacted February 9, 2026
findings.summary: The DIFC Authority enacted the Variable Capital Company Regulations on February 9, 2026, introducing a flexible corporate vehicle for proprietary investment activities. A VCC may be standalone or umbrella (with incorporated or segregated cells), can distribute capital (not just profits), has share capital equal to net asset value, and may qualify for 0% corporate tax as a Qualifying Free Zone Person. VCCs must appoint a Corporate Service Provider but do not require DFSA authorisation unless engaging in regulated activities.
findings.client_impact: The VCC is a new flexible proprietary investment vehicle in the DIFC — capable of capital distributions, umbrella structures with segregated cells, and a potential 0% tax rate as a Qualifying Free Zone Person. This is a material new structuring option for clients building UAE-domiciled family investment offices, multi-class holding vehicles, or segregated wealth portfolios.
findings.urgency_reason: WATCH because the VCC is newly available and represents a genuine planning opportunity that FGC's UAE-connected HNW clients may not yet have considered; early engagement allows clients to establish VCCs as the regulatory framework matures.

findings.title: ADGM tightens AML and crypto rules; DIFC opens prescribed company regime to all applicants
findings.summary: The ADGM Financial Services Regulatory Authority (FSRA) has finalized tightened AML obligations and a new regulatory framework for the staking of virtual assets. Separately, the DIFC opened its Prescribed Company (PC) regime to all applicants (previously restricted), enabling a wider range of international structures to be registered in the DIFC. ADGM's FSRA Consultation Paper No. 12 of 2025 also proposes a lighter regime for smaller fund managers (under USD 200M committed capital).
findings.client_impact: ADGM-licensed wealth managers and advisors must update their AML frameworks in line with FSRA's finalized tightened obligations, while the fully open DIFC Prescribed Company regime now provides a cost-effective UAE-registered holding structure available to any international structure — previously only accessible to certain categories. Clients with UAE holding needs should assess the PC as a low-cost, DIFC-registered option.
findings.urgency_reason: WATCH because ADGM AML tightening requires existing licensees to update compliance policies, and the open DIFC PC regime creates a planning opportunity for clients who were previously ineligible.

findings.title: Panama advancing economic substance requirements — KPMG proposal under review 2026
findings.summary: KPMG Panama released a client alert in 2026 outlining a proposal for economic substance requirements for certain Panamanian legal entities, aligning Panama more closely with OECD and FATF standards. Panama already maintains a centralized Beneficial Ownership Registry (Law 129 of 2020) accessible to competent authorities. Recent reforms also include stricter accounting record obligations (Bill 624) requiring five-year retention and updated transfer pricing standards.
findings.client_impact: If enacted, economic substance requirements would require clients with Panama holding companies to establish genuine operational presence (local directors, activities, and expenditure) in Panama, potentially making the jurisdiction significantly less attractive as a pure offshore holding location. Clients should flag this risk to their Panama counsel and assess contingency structures now rather than waiting for final legislation.
findings.urgency_reason: WATCH because the proposal is under active review and Panama faces ongoing OECD/FATF pressure to enact substance rules; proactive monitoring allows clients to reposition structures during any transition period rather than under a compliance deadline.

findings.title: Bahamas preparing for CFATF Fifth Round Mutual Evaluation; onsite visit scheduled October 2026
findings.summary: The Bahamas Financial Stability Council has confirmed that 2026 is a critical year for AML/CFT compliance as the jurisdiction prepares for the Caribbean Financial Action Task Force (CFATF) Fifth Round Mutual Evaluation onsite visit scheduled October 19–30, 2026. The evaluation will assess effectiveness across all FATF Recommendations. The Central Bank also published revised Application Guidelines for Private Trust Companies, Qualified Executive Entities, and Registered Representatives.
findings.client_impact: A poor CFATF evaluation outcome could result in the Bahamas being placed on a FATF grey or watch list, which would sharply increase the due diligence burden for all counterparties transacting with Bahamas-connected structures and could impair banking relationships for Bahamas trusts and entities. Clients with Bahamas private trust companies, trusts, or holding structures should be aware and consider alternative jurisdictions as a contingency.
findings.urgency_reason: WATCH because the onsite evaluation is approximately 3.5 months away; a negative outcome post-October could rapidly change the KYC environment for Bahamas structures, and advance awareness allows time to plan alternatives if the evaluation is unfavorable.

findings.title: Trusts (Jersey) Amendment Law 2026 modernises the 1984 Trusts Law
findings.summary: The Trusts (Jersey) Amendment Law 2026 has been passed, updating the foundational Trusts (Jersey) Law 1984 based on recommendations from the Trusts Law Working Group. The amendments bring greater clarity and certainty for trustees, beneficiaries, and lenders, reinforcing Jersey's position as a leading trust jurisdiction. Specific improvements include modernised provisions on trustee powers, protector roles, and lending against trust assets.
findings.client_impact: Updated provisions on trustee powers, protector roles, and lending against trust assets make Jersey trusts more flexible for sophisticated succession and wealth planning, particularly for clients seeking to leverage trust assets for investment financing. Existing Jersey trust deeds may benefit from a deed of variation to incorporate the improved provisions and take advantage of the modernised framework.
findings.urgency_reason: WATCH because the updated law is newly in force and represents both a planning opportunity and a review trigger; advisors with Jersey trust clients should assess whether existing trust deeds should be updated to incorporate the new statutory provisions.

findings.title: OECD Pillar Two 'Side-by-Side' package agreed January 5, 2026: four new safe harbors for 15% global minimum tax
findings.summary: On January 5, 2026, 147 OECD Inclusive Framework jurisdictions agreed on the 'Side-by-Side' package of new Administrative Guidance for the Pillar Two Global Minimum Tax. The package introduces four new safe harbors and extends an existing safe harbor, allowing the US minimum tax system (GILTI/CAMT) to coexist alongside Pillar Two. The first GloBE Information Return (GIR) filings for calendar-year taxpayers were due June 30, 2026, with OECD guidance on the GIR XML Schema released in June 2026.
findings.client_impact: Multinational groups with EUR 750M+ consolidated revenue — or HNW clients who are part of such groups through operating businesses — face live GloBE Information Return obligations, with first filings due June 30, 2026. The Side-by-Side safe harbors are critical for US-headquartered clients with offshore structures: advisors should confirm whether GILTI/CAMT satisfies the client's Pillar Two obligations and whether any GIR filings are overdue.
findings.urgency_reason: WATCH because the first GIR filing deadline passed June 30, 2026 (5 days ago), and any clients in scope who have not filed should be urgently engaged. The new safe harbors also require analysis to confirm applicability to US-connected structures.

findings.title: Bahamas Usufruct Interest Act 2026 enacted March 23: first comprehensive statutory usufruct framework in a common law jurisdiction
findings.summary: The Bahamas enacted the Usufruct Interest Act 2026 (Act No. 9 of 2026) on March 23, 2026, establishing the first comprehensive statutory usufruct framework in a common law jurisdiction. The Act allows formal separation of the right to use, enjoy, and derive income from an asset from its legal ownership, covering equity interests, securities, IP, digital assets, receivables, and insurance policies. Usufructs may last up to 99 years for natural persons and 30 years for legal entities.
findings.client_impact: The Usufruct Act provides a new statutory vehicle to separate economic rights (income, use) from legal ownership across a broad range of assets including digital assets — an innovative tool for succession planning, asset protection, and intergenerational transfer without legal title change. This is particularly valuable for clients with mixed civil/common law family situations, forced heirship concerns, or digital asset succession planning needs.
findings.urgency_reason: WATCH because this is a newly enacted statute representing a genuinely innovative structuring opportunity; clients engaged in succession planning or asset protection discussions should be briefed on this new Bahamian tool.

findings.title: MAS introduces formal notification framework for Single-Family Offices, replacing bespoke exemptions
findings.summary: The Monetary Authority of Singapore has introduced a new framework for single-family offices (SFOs), moving from case-by-case bespoke exemptions to a formal notification process. The framework formalizes the conditions under which SFOs may manage assets for a single family without holding a capital markets services licence, bringing greater regulatory certainty while maintaining proportionate oversight.
findings.client_impact: Singapore-based single-family offices can now operate under a formal notification regime rather than seeking bespoke CMS licence exemptions on a case-by-case basis, providing greater regulatory certainty and a clearer compliance pathway. Clients with existing Singapore SFOs operating under old bespoke arrangements should assess whether migration to the new notification framework is required or beneficial.
findings.urgency_reason: WATCH because the framework change is recent and shifts how SFOs obtain and maintain their regulatory status in Singapore; existing SFOs and clients considering establishing a Singapore SFO should engage with MAS's new notification process without delay.

findings.title: UAE Capital Market Authority replaces Securities and Commodities Authority from January 1, 2026
findings.summary: The UAE's Capital Market Authority (CMA) replaced the Securities and Commodities Authority (SCA) effective January 1, 2026 under a new regulatory framework for capital markets. This represents a significant restructuring of the UAE's mainland capital markets regulatory architecture, distinct from the DIFC (DFSA) and ADGM (FSRA) free zone regulators.
findings.client_impact: Clients with UAE mainland investment structures, securities registrations, or regulatory approvals previously governed by the SCA should confirm whether their existing licences, registrations, and approvals have been automatically transferred to the CMA or require re-registration. Operating on lapsed or unvalidated approvals post-restructuring creates regulatory exposure.
findings.urgency_reason: WATCH because the transition occurred January 1, 2026, and clients who have not confirmed their regulatory status under the new CMA may be operating on unvalidated approvals now six months into the new framework.

findings.title: BCB VASP regulatory framework fully operational from February 2026
findings.summary: BCB Resolutions 519, 520, and 521 established a comprehensive regulatory regime for Virtual Asset Service Providers (VASPs) in Brazil, effective February 2, 2026. The framework integrates certain VASP activities — particularly international payments and transfers using virtual assets — into Brazil's existing foreign exchange regime. VASPs must now be authorized and are subject to BCB oversight.
findings.client_impact: Brazil-based VASPs are now BCB-regulated, meaning clients using VASPs for cross-border transactions involving Brazil must confirm their VASP counterparty is properly authorized. The framework also affects due diligence on Brazil-connected virtual asset structures.
findings.urgency_reason: FYI because the framework has been in force since February 2026; no immediate client action is required today, but advisors should note the regulatory backdrop when structuring Brazil cross-border flows involving virtual assets.

findings.title: FIRPTA Form 8288 updated January 2026: direct deposit lines and Section 1446(f) coverage added
findings.summary: The IRS revised Form 8288 in January 2026, adding lines for direct deposit of refunds claimed under FIRPTA withholding. The form was also expanded to cover partnership interest transfers under Section 1446(f), broadening its scope beyond real property disposals. The IRS will mandate EFTPS electronic payment for all FIRPTA withholding.
findings.client_impact: Foreign clients selling US real estate or partnership interests subject to Section 1446(f) must use the updated Form 8288 for withholding and refund claims, now supporting direct deposit refunds and EFTPS payment. Advisors and closing agents should ensure the current form version is used for all transactions from January 2026 onwards to avoid IRS processing errors.
findings.urgency_reason: FYI — administrative procedural update affecting form compliance for foreign sellers of US real property or partnership interests; no new substantive tax obligation is introduced.

findings.title: Florida SB 264 foreign real estate buyer restrictions upheld; near-total ban on Chinese nationals remains in force
findings.summary: Florida's Senate Bill 264 (effective July 1, 2023) was upheld by courts in November 2025 and remains operative in 2026. It imposes near-total restrictions on Chinese nationals acquiring any Florida real estate, and restricts nationals of six other 'countries of concern' from purchasing agricultural land or property within 10 miles of military/critical infrastructure. All buyers must sign compliance affidavits; violations risk forfeiture and criminal charges.
findings.client_impact: FGC clients who are Chinese nationals face a near-total prohibition on acquiring any Florida real estate, and nationals of Russia, Iran, North Korea, Cuba, Venezuela, or Syria face restrictions near military and critical infrastructure sites. All such clients must be advised before entering any Florida purchase contract, and compliance affidavits are mandatory at closing.
findings.urgency_reason: FYI because the law has been in force since 2023 and was judicially upheld in 2025; this is a standing compliance reminder for any transaction with affected-nationality clients, not a new development requiring immediate action.

findings.title: Cayman Islands tokenised funds framework in force from March 24, 2026
findings.summary: The Mutual Funds (Amendment) Act 2026, Private Funds (Amendment) Act 2026, and Virtual Asset (Service Providers) (Amendment) Act 2026 came into force March 24, 2026, introducing a dedicated regulatory framework for tokenised investment funds. Tokenised funds are regulated under the existing CIMA funds framework, not as VASPs. CIMA has supervisory authority over token transactions, and all token issuance/transfer records must be available for CIMA inspection.
findings.client_impact: Cayman investment funds wishing to tokenise their fund interests now have a clear regulatory pathway under existing CIMA oversight — without requiring separate VASP registration. Clients exploring digital-native fund structures or existing funds considering tokenisation of LP interests have regulatory clarity to proceed.
findings.urgency_reason: FYI — no compliance burden for existing non-tokenised Cayman funds; relevant background for clients exploring next-generation fund structures or digital asset fund strategies.

findings.title: Luxembourg SPF may now be organized as a simplified joint-stock company (SAS)
findings.summary: The Luxembourg law of December 19, 2025 amended the SPF law to allow private wealth management companies (SPFs) to be organized as simplified joint-stock companies (sociétés par actions simplifiées). Previously, SPFs were limited to SA, SARL, cooperative, or corporate partnership forms. SPFs remain exempt from Luxembourg income tax, wealth tax, and municipal income tax.
findings.client_impact: HNW clients structuring Luxembourg private wealth holding vehicles now have the SAS as an additional form option, offering greater governance flexibility and simplified shareholder arrangements compared to the SA. New or restructuring Luxembourg SPF arrangements should evaluate whether the SAS form better matches the client's ownership and governance objectives.
findings.urgency_reason: FYI — planning opportunity now available; no compliance deadline, but worth reviewing for clients who are establishing or restructuring Luxembourg SPF arrangements.

findings.title: Luxembourg carried interest tax reform: preferential regime applicable from 2026 tax year
findings.summary: A new carried interest tax regime, effective for the 2026 tax year, broadens eligible beneficiaries, specifies covered arrangement types, and establishes a permanent preferential regime. Only the outperformance component of carried interest is taxed as a speculative gain and may be exempt from personal income tax if specific holding and service conditions are met.
findings.client_impact: Fund managers receiving carried interest from Luxembourg-domiciled structures may benefit from personal income tax exemption on the outperformance component under the new permanent regime, subject to holding and service conditions. FGC clients who are fund managers or key investment professionals receiving Luxembourg carry should review eligibility with their Luxembourg tax advisors.
findings.urgency_reason: FYI — the regime applies from the 2026 tax year and is relevant for fund manager clients; no immediate action deadline, but structure reviews should be undertaken before 2026 year-end to confirm eligibility.

findings.title: Luxembourg DAC 8 transposition expected early 2026: new reporting for crypto-asset service providers
findings.summary: Luxembourg is in the process of transposing the EU DAC 8 directive, which introduces due diligence and reporting obligations for crypto-asset service providers and extends the Common Reporting Standard (CRS) to cover crypto assets. Transposition is anticipated to be completed in 2026, imposing new obligations on Luxembourg-based crypto service providers to report client account information for automatic exchange.
findings.client_impact: Clients holding crypto assets through Luxembourg-based service providers should expect account information to become subject to CRS-style automatic exchange reporting once DAC 8 is transposed. This expands the reporting perimeter beyond traditional financial accounts to crypto holdings in Luxembourg, with implications for tax transparency across the EU.
findings.urgency_reason: FYI — transposition is in progress but specific effective reporting dates have not been confirmed; relevant for crypto-adjacent Luxembourg structures and clients who rely on Luxembourg crypto service providers for asset custody.

findings.title: Switzerland CARF implementation: legal framework in force January 2026 but data exchange delayed to 2028 at earliest
findings.summary: Switzerland's domestic legal framework for the OECD Crypto-Asset Reporting Framework (CARF) entered into force January 1, 2026. However, the Federal Council decided that crypto due diligence obligations will not apply in 2026 due to ongoing alignment with 74 partner jurisdictions on data security. The earliest possible first data exchange is now 2028.
findings.client_impact: Swiss-connected clients holding crypto assets through Swiss reporting providers have until at least 2028 before CARF data exchange with other jurisdictions begins — providing meaningful lead time to plan and structure crypto holdings before Swiss CARF reporting becomes effective — despite the domestic legal framework already being in place.
findings.urgency_reason: FYI — the two-year delay on data exchange removes near-term reporting pressure; the information is useful for advisors planning Swiss crypto structures, but no immediate client compliance action is required.

findings.title: Zurich cuts cantonal wealth tax multiplier to 95% in 2026 — largest reduction in 20 years
findings.summary: For the 2026 tax year, the canton of Zurich reduced its cantonal multiplier to 95%, the biggest cantonal wealth tax reduction in two decades. Lucerne dropped to 1.45 units and Schwyz trimmed to 110%. Switzerland's wealth tax is levied at the cantonal and municipal level (approximately 0.1–0.8% per year on net assets depending on canton), with no federal wealth tax.
findings.client_impact: Switzerland's competitive position for HNW residency and asset domicile has improved materially in 2026, particularly in Zurich, Lucerne, and Schwyz. Clients comparing Swiss holding costs against other jurisdictions or considering Swiss residency for wealth consolidation should update their cantonal tax comparisons with the new multipliers.
findings.urgency_reason: FYI — the rate changes apply to the 2026 tax year and are a useful data point for client residency and structuring discussions; no compliance action required.

findings.title: MAS Circular AMLD 05/2026: risk-proportionate Source of Wealth standards for wealth management FIs
findings.summary: On May 25, 2026, MAS issued Circular AMLD 05/2026, setting out how financial institutions should establish a customer's Source of Wealth (SOW) in a risk-proportionate way. The circular introduces risk-tiered documentation requirements, simplified verification for established client referrals, and accelerated processes for clients with transparent wealth structures. MAS described prior industry practices as 'unnecessary and excessive.'
findings.client_impact: Singapore financial institutions must now apply risk-tiered SOW standards rather than applying maximum documentation requirements uniformly — meaning FGC clients with transparent wealth structures or established referral relationships should experience significantly faster and less burdensome Singapore onboarding. Advisors preparing client packages for Singapore accounts should calibrate documentation to the MAS risk tier, not over-document.
findings.urgency_reason: FYI — favorable regulatory guidance already issued; no compliance deadline, but useful for advisors preparing Singapore onboarding documentation and client briefings on what to expect from Singapore institutions.

findings.title: MFSA Annual Report 2025 published July 1, 2026; DeFi Discussion Paper consultation closes July 10
findings.summary: The Malta Financial Services Authority published its Annual Report 2025 on July 1, 2026, covering 1,849 supervisory interactions, 1,023 authorisations, and €570,673 in penalties. Separately, the MFSA's Discussion Paper on Decentralised Finance (DeFi), published June 12, 2026 (reference 03-2026), is open for public consultation until July 10, 2026.
findings.client_impact: The MFSA's DeFi Discussion Paper (consultation closes July 10) signals Malta's ambition to develop a regulatory framework for decentralised finance, which could create new regulated pathways for clients with DeFi exposure or strategies. The Annual Report's supervisory statistics indicate an active regulator with significant enforcement activity.
findings.urgency_reason: FYI — the consultation deadline is July 10 (4 days away) but participation is not a client compliance obligation; relevant background for FGC if it wishes to contribute to Malta's DeFi regulatory framework or for clients considering Malta as a DeFi jurisdiction.

findings.title: MFSA 2026 supervisory priorities: financial crime compliance a key pillar alongside consumer protection and cross-border supervision
findings.summary: The MFSA has confirmed its seven supervisory pillars for 2026: resilience, sustainable finance, digital finance, governance/risk/compliance, financial crime compliance, consumer protection, and cross-border supervision. Financial crime compliance is receiving heightened attention alongside CRR III / CRD VI transposition.
findings.client_impact: Malta-regulated entities in FGC's client ecosystem should expect heightened MFSA scrutiny on AML/KYC compliance, governance, and cross-border activities throughout 2026 as part of the MFSA's declared supervisory priorities. Clients with Malta-regulated structures should ensure their compliance frameworks are current before any supervisory interaction.
findings.urgency_reason: FYI — supervisory priority disclosure provides advance notice of MFSA focus areas; no immediate action deadline but useful for advising Malta-licensed clients on where to concentrate their 2026 compliance investment.

findings.title: Cyprus mandates traceable banking for rent payments over €500 from July 2026
findings.summary: As part of the 2026 Cyprus tax reform package, from July 2026, rent payments exceeding €500 must be made through traceable banking methods. This measure aims to improve tax compliance in the real estate rental sector. Violations expose landlords to administrative penalties.
findings.client_impact: Clients with Cyprus investment properties who receive rent payments above €500/month must now use traceable banking channels — cash payments of this size are no longer permissible. Property managers handling rent collection for Cyprus landlords should confirm their payment processes comply with the new requirement to avoid penalties.
findings.urgency_reason: FYI — the requirement took effect in July 2026 (current month) and is a compliance matter for Cyprus landlords; low urgency for most FGC clients but applicable to those with Cyprus rental portfolios.

findings.title: FATF updates high-risk jurisdiction list June 19, 2026: Iran, DPRK, and Burma subject to call for action (unchanged)
findings.summary: On June 19, 2026, FATF updated its list of High-Risk Jurisdictions subject to a Call for Action. The list is unchanged from the prior update, with Iran, DPRK, and Burma remaining as jurisdictions subject to calls for action. FATF also amended its Consolidated Processes and Procedures for Mutual Evaluations.
findings.client_impact: The FATF high-risk list remains unchanged: clients whose structures, counterparties, or transaction chains touch Iran, DPRK, or Burma continue to face maximum AML/KYC scrutiny and material banking relationship risk. No new jurisdictions have been added, which confirms no new elevated-risk exposures since the prior update.
findings.urgency_reason: FYI — routine update confirming no change to the list; confirms baseline AML compliance standing for all FGC client structures and provides confirmation that no previously clean jurisdiction has moved to high-risk status.

findings.title: FATCA XML Schema v2.0.1 now primary standard; UK CRS 2.0 from 2026 reporting year
findings.summary: The IRS's FATCA XML Schema v2.0.1 became the primary standard for FATCA reporting effective October 6, 2025. From January 1, 2027, all FATCA XML returns must use v2.0.1. The UK's CRS 2.0 regime, effective from the 2026 reporting year (returns due May 31, 2027), adds new data points including account type, joint account flag, and controlling person roles. UK FATCA and CRS reporting are being separated into distinct schemas.
findings.client_impact: Compliance teams and reporting agents at Cayman or UK financial institutions must migrate to FATCA XML Schema v2.0.1 by January 1, 2027, and UK CRS 2.0 reporting will require new data fields for the 2026 reporting year (returns due May 31, 2027). Clients with FI reporting obligations in these jurisdictions should confirm their technology providers are scoping the required system upgrades now.
findings.urgency_reason: FYI — future deadlines (January 2027 for FATCA XML; May 2027 for UK CRS 2.0); no immediate action required today, but advisors should flag to compliance technology vendors to begin upgrade planning to avoid a last-minute crunch.
